Hemp: A Game Changer for the Building Industry
===============

Hemp: A Game Changer for the Building Industry
==============================================

![Image 1: Cambridgeshire with hempcrete panels](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/inside-flat-house-cambridgeshire-with-hempcrete-panels-exposed.jpeg)

Inside Flat House, Cambridgeshire with hempcrete panels exposed — Oskar Proctor

In order to reduce carbon emissions, the building industry is in high demand for sustainable materials. Approximately [37% of energy- and process-related worldwide carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xas8R7dVJrs) are attributable to buildings and construction. This is because the sector heavily relies on fossil fuels, uses carbon-intensive building materials like concrete, steel, and glass, and emits [greenhouse gases](https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20230309-how-carbon-negative-concrete-can-help-the-climate) to heat and cool our structures.

Therefore, as an alternative to other building materials such as concrete, hemp is rapidly becoming one of the go — to sustainable building materials, here’s why.

But Health First!🌿
-------------------

Traditional building materials often contain toxic substances like formaldehyde and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Hemp offers an alternative through the production of hempcrete. Hempcrete, a bio-composite material made from hemp hurds and lime, is [non-toxic](https://www.getforevergreen.com/blog-old/2023/6/1/six-ways-hemp-is-helping-create-a-non-toxic-world#:~:text=Healthy%20Building%20Materials&text=Hempcrete%2C%20a%20bio%2Dcomposite%20material,promotes%20a%20healthier%20indoor%20environment), breathable, and has excellent insulation properties. Using hempcrete in construction reduces our exposure to harmful chemicals and promotes a healthier indoor environment.

Time…Our Most Valuable Asset⏱️
------------------------------

Since there are no cement seams or drying times like with regular concrete blocks, a building made with ready-to-use hemp blocks can be finished 20 to 30 percent faster than the average manufacturing schedule. What’s cool about hemp blocks is that they are simple to assemble they simply fit together like Legos which results in fewer employees being needed on the job site.

![Image 2: Hempblocks - app.2050-materials.com](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Hemp_block.png)

Hempblocks — [app.2050-materials.com](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/details_designer/the-hemp-blocks-company-ltd-hempcrete-insulation-blocks-blocco-ambiente-hb400-36x40x20/)

Below are some newly added hemp products on our [platform](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/results?materialtype=40).

![Image 3: Hemp building materials displayed on the 2050 Materials platform.](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Group-765-scaled.webp)

[Natur-Chanv/Hemp Insulating Panel](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/details_designer/nature-fibres-inc-panneau-isolant-natur-chanv-hemp/)&[Hemp Fibre](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/details_designer/ekolution-ab-hemp-fibre/)

![Image 4: Newly added hemp products showcased.](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Group-767-scaled.webp)

[Thermo Hanf Stuffing Wool](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/details_designer/thermo-hanf-thermo-hanf-stuffing-wool/)&[ADEPT External Wall System](https://app.2050-materials.com/product/details_designer/natural-building-systems-external-wall-system-u-value-0-26/)

A By-product of Agriculture 🌾
------------------------------

The production of hemp is unusually high compared to other agricultural crops. According to a US Department of Agriculture research, [worldwide yields of dry straw per acre ranged from 2.5 to 8.7 tons](https://newsociety.com/blogs/news/the-benefits-of-using-hempcrete-in-your-construction-projects?sitedomain=row&_ga=2.118001932.403297836.1690458040-305658927.1690458040). This is an advantageous comparison to wheat straw yields of 1.25 to 2.5 tons per acre. No other plant offers as much biomass as hemp does in terms of the volume that may be used from a single harvest.

![Image 5: Hemp agriculture image by FreePik](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Screen-Shot-2023-08-17-at-3.56.43-PM.png)

Hemp agriculture image by [FreePik](https://www.freepik.com/free-photo/portrait-senior-agronomist-looking-hemp-cannabis-plants-field-cannabis-sativa-plant_11135361.htm#query=hemp%20agriculture&position=14&from_view=search&track=ais)

Hemp's Unique Properties 🫧
---------------------------

Hempcrete is a totally permeable building material, meaning it has fully open vapour transmission even while it will prevent liquid water from going through the wall. The gaseous water that flows through buildings as humidity and steam is able to flow into the wall, pass through it, and escape on the other side without really condensing or having a dew point form within the body of the wall itself. This makes it the perfect material for rainy and temperature-variable climates since these natural structures produce a passive humidity control system that lowers the buildup of moisture, thus reducing the occurrence of issues like moulds and rot. Additionally, it helps to produce thermal inertia, a closed system in which water vapor serves as a heat sink to stop the building from being heated or cooled.

In comparison to conventional insulation materials, polyurethane-hemp fiber composites have “good insulating properties,” according to a 2020 [study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214509517302218) on the thermal properties of hemp.

![Image 6: Hempcrete properties by Issuu](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Screen-Shot-2023-07-31-at-2.57.32-PM.png)

Hempcrete properties by [Issuu](https://issuu.com/337media/docs/420_issue_digital_upload/s/10452882)

In an effort to encourage designers to research and implement other alternatives of insulation materials in their projects, we created Illustrated Guides to Insulation Materials that offer visual guides to understand the variety of insulation materials, their carbon and thermal performance and their classification per resource origin Hit the download button and check them out.

[Download](https://2050materials.gumroad.com/l/xaypkf)

Diverse Environmental Advantages ♻️
-----------------------------------

The degree to which Hempcrete reduces energy consumption in the buildings it is used to construct, drastically lowering heating and cooling costs during the lifecycle of a building, is one of its main environmental benefits. There are benefits for industry as well such as emitting way fewer greenhouse gases than concrete.

Hemp’s ability to capture carbon could make building more environmentally friendly, assisting nations in achieving their net zero goals.

![Image 7](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/themes/2050materials/assets/img/quote.svg) "Optimizing the way we design, build, and renovate our buildings has a critical role to play in driving down emissions and enabling the UK to meet its climate targets." - Yetunde Abdul, head of climate action at the UK Green Building Council 

Main Types of Building Products Made of Hemp
--------------------------------------------

Hemp has been utilized in the production of various building materials. These materials take advantage of hemp’s strong fibers and environmentally friendly characteristics. Some building products made from hemp include:

*   **_Hempcrete:_**_Hempcrete is a bio-composite material made from a mixture of hemp hurds (the woody inner core of the hemp plant), lime, and water. It is used as a lightweight and insulating construction material for walls, floors, and roofs._
*   **_Hemp Fiberboards:_**_Hemp fibers can be processed and compressed to create fiberboards used for paneling, insulation, and flooring. They are a sustainable alternative to conventional fiberboards made from wood._
*   **_Hemp Insulation:_**_Hemp fibers can be used as an insulating material in buildings to reduce energy consumption and provide a more environmentally friendly option compared to traditional insulation materials._

*   **_Hemp Plastics:_**_Hemp fibers can be combined with other materials to create bioplastics, which can be used for various building components such as window frames, roofing tiles, and structural elements._

*   **_Hemp Carpets and Textiles:_**_Hemp fibers can be woven into durable carpets and textiles used for interior decoration and furnishings._
*   **_Hemp Roofing Materials:_**_Some companies have developed roofing shingles and tiles made from hemp fibers, providing an eco-friendly roofing option._
*   **_Hemp Wallboards:_**_Hemp fibers can be used to make wallboards that are suitable for interior partitions and ceilings._
*   **_Hemp Oil-Based Finishes:_**_Hemp oil is used as a natural finishing product for wood and other surfaces in buildings._

It’s important to note however that the availability and use of these hemp-based building products may vary depending on regional regulations, local industries, and advancements in technology.

The Growing Use of Hempcrete
----------------------------

Despite being mostly used in Europe and North America, the use of hempcrete is expanding due to the relaxation of rules and growing awareness of the substance’s numerous advantages.

Adopting environmentally friendly building materials, like hemp, could contribute to a change toward a construction methodology that makes use of natural resources that are acquired locally. Which includes capturing and storing carbon in buildings, as well as getting the majority of your building mass from fast-turnover crops as a way to provide low-carbon insulation.

In the new Phoenix Project in Lewes, East Sussex, Material Cultures intends to keep employing hemp. The concept, which was created by Human Nature and is intended to be a 700-home neighborhood constructed with sustainability at its core, will use hempcrete and locally sourced timber in the majority of the buildings.

![Image 8: Pheonix Project housing - Material Cultures](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/phoenix-project-housing-in-lewes-takes-hemp-and-gormley-up-a-scale.jpeg)

Pheonix Project housing — Material Cultures

![Image 9](https://2050-materials.com/wp-content/themes/2050materials/assets/img/quote.svg) "There is no one solution to save the world, but for now, hempcrete remains a positive option." Thibaut Barrault - Co-founder of Barrault Pressacco

--------------------------

# HEMPCRETE: Innovation and Sustainability in Construction

![HEMPCRETE: Innovación y Sostenibilidad en la Construcción](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201000%201000'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![HEMPCRETE: Innovación y Sostenibilidad en la Construcción](//www.ekohunters.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/HEMPCRETE-portada.jpg)

As previously mentioned in the [EkoBlog](https://www.ekohunters.com/ekoblog), the construction industry is one of the most polluting sectors globally, responsible for 23% of air pollution and 40% of drinking water contamination. This reality has led the construction industry to face the challenge of adopting more responsible practices.

[HEMPCRETE](https://www.hempcrete.es/), an innovative company specializing in the manufacturing and marketing of hemp-based construction materials, stands as a benchmark in this paradigm shift. Founded by Raquel Sanchis, an architect trained at the prestigious Pratt Institute in New York, and her father Fernando Sanchis, a lime expert with over five decades of experience, HEMPCRETE merges tradition and modernity in its construction approach. In this new article from the EkoBlog, we introduce HEMPCRETE, its fundamental principles, and the uniqueness of its products. Read on to learn about its history.

## History and Foundations of HEMPCRETE

### Origins of a Sustainable Vision

The story of [HEMPCRETE](https://www.hempcrete.es/) is forged at the intersection of traditional construction and sustainable innovation. Raquel Sanchis, after years of training and specialization in sustainable design and bioclimatic architecture, conceived the idea of creating a company that not only addressed energy efficiency in construction but also utilized renewable resources. Together with her father, whose extensive experience in traditional lime construction provided invaluable technical knowledge, HEMPCRETE was established with a clear objective: to redefine the standards of bioconstruction through the use of materials that are both innovative and environmentally friendly.

The choice of hemp as the central raw material is not coincidental. This fast-growing crop requires minimal water resources and has become a key ally in the pursuit of sustainable construction solutions. HEMPCRETE relies on research highlighting the exceptional properties of hemp, promoting its use as an efficient and eco-friendly construction material.

### Mission and Vision

HEMPCRETE articulates its mission around transforming the construction industry by promoting the use of natural materials that not only meet technical standards but also ensure a comfortable and healthy interior environment. The company’s vision is ambitious: to become a benchmark in sustainable construction, creating spaces that care for both people and the planet. This holistic approach seeks to inspire a change in construction practices, emphasizing the need to build consciously.

![HEMPCRETE: Innovación y Sostenibilidad en la Construcción](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20700'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![HEMPCRETE: Innovación y Sostenibilidad en la Construcción](//www.ekohunters.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/HEMPCRETE-3.jpg)

# Subscribe and receive all the news directly in your mailbox

## Products and Market Differentiation

### Innovation in Construction Materials

[HEMPCRETE](https://www.hempcrete.es/) is distinguished by its wide range of products, all made from hemp derivatives. Its flagship product, hemp concrete prefabricated blocks, consists of three natural ingredients: hemp, lime, and water. This simplified design not only optimizes the construction process but also guarantees superior thermal and acoustic performance. With a thermal conductivity of 0.07 W/m·K, these blocks provide an effective solution for meeting the requirements of the Technical Building Code (CTE) in various climatic zones in Spain, eliminating the need for additional layers of insulation.

HEMPCRETE also supplies hemp shiv, a versatile material that allows for the on-site production of hemp concrete, adapting to the project’s specifications. Additionally, its hemp fiber panels are used as insulation systems in roofs, walls, and floors, contributing to interior comfort and energy efficiency.

### Competitive Advantages

HEMPCRETE’s differentiation in the market lies in its dual focus on biomaterials and conventional construction materials. Unlike many competitors, HEMPCRETE offers industrialized products that ensure a constant supply and consistent quality, facilitating their application in large-scale projects. Furthermore, its hemp concrete not only meets ecological requirements but also boasts notable properties: its negative carbon footprint of -75 kg of CO₂ per cubic meter makes it a highly desirable material for initiatives seeking sustainability certifications.

Another highlight is the natural moisture resistance of its products and their high fire resistance rating (B-s1, d0). These characteristics not only ensure the safety of buildings but also contribute to the creation of healthy environments free from toxic compounds.

![HEMPCRETE: Innovation and Sustainability in Construction](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20700'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![HEMPCRETE: Innovation and Sustainability in Construction](//www.ekohunters.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/HEMPCRETE-2.jpg)

## Sustainability and Product Life Cycle

[HEMPCRETE](https://www.hempcrete.es/) ensures the sustainability of its products through the use of hemp cultivated under ecological agricultural practices, which involves lower water consumption and no pesticides. Throughout its life cycle, hemp concrete acts as a carbon sink, contributing to climate change mitigation. The ability of hemp to regenerate soil and its rapid growth, in just 3-4 months, makes it an ideal option in the pursuit of responsible agricultural practices.

HEMPCRETE products are designed for long life, and at the end of their cycle, they are completely recyclable and biodegradable, ensuring they do not generate harmful waste. This comprehensive approach ensures that every stage of their life, from production to final disposal, minimizes environmental impact.

![HEMPCRETE: Innovation and Sustainability in Construction](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20700'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![HEMPCRETE: Innovation and Sustainability in Construction](//www.ekohunters.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/HEMPCRETE-1.jpg)

[HEMPCRETE](https://www.hempcrete.es/) stands as a leader at the intersection of innovation and sustainability in the construction sector. Its focus on bioconstruction and the use of hemp-derived materials not only responds to current market demands but also anticipates a more responsible future in building. With products that guarantee energy efficiency and superior quality, HEMPCRETE not only constructs buildings but also builds a legacy toward a more sustainable environment. # Hempcrete Offers Path to Greener Construction Industry

## 

#### By [Maeve Arthur](/authors/maeve-arthur)

June 30, 2020

|  |
| --- |
| **Highland Hemp House in Bellingham, WA,  after the hempcrete has been cast.**   ([Credit: Tommy Gibbons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Highland_Hemp_House_finished_hempcrete.jpg)) |

![](/images/content/Highland_Hemp_House_finished_hempcrete-300.jpg)  
**Highland Hemp House in Bellingham, WA,  
after the hempcrete has been cast.**   
([Credit: Tommy Gibbons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Highland_Hemp_House_finished_hempcrete.jpg))

Look around you: how much of your surrounding environment was built using concrete? For many of us, the answer is most of it.

Concrete is the most widely used construction material in the world. It is also a long-standing source of environmental degradation and human health complications. Around the world, more than 4 billion tons of concrete are produced each year, accounting for nearly [8 percent](https://www.chathamhouse.org/sites/default/files/publications/2018-06-13-making-concrete-change-cement-lehne-preston-final.pdf) of global CO2 emissions. Concrete production stresses global water supplies as well: a 2018 study in [*Nature Sustainability*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41893-017-0009-5) found that the concrete industry accounts for nearly one-tenth of all industry water withdrawals, and 1.7 percent of all global water withdrawals. Concrete production poses significant human health risks primarily from respirable crystalline silica (RCS) dust—tiny particles of the mineral crystalline silica—created during heavy industrial processes such as cutting, grinding, sawing or crushing concrete. According to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration ([OSHA](https://www.osha.gov/dsg/topics/silicacrystalline/)), the 2.3 million Americans exposed to RCS dust at work are vulnerable to silicosis, lung cancer, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and kidney disease.

Due to these environmental and human health risks, some experts are promoting [hempcrete](https://nesea.org/be-event/hempcrete-101-back-future-natural-carbon-beneficial-buildings), or hemplime, as the future of the construction industry. Hempcrete is a bio-composite walling and insulation material that is used to construct and retrofit buildings. As its name suggests, hempcrete is made from the hemp plant. Hempcrete processors combine hemp hurd (the woody core of the hemp plant stem) with a lime-based binder and water to create hempcrete. The use of hempcrete for homes and commercial buildings began in France in the 1980s and soon spread to other European nations such as Belgium, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom.

Unlike concrete, hempcrete offers numerous environmental and human health benefits. [HempStone LLC](https://hempstone.net/), a U.S. hempcrete company, notes that hempcrete is healthy, resilient, and sequesters carbon. It is non-toxic, biodegradable, anti-fungal, anti-pest, fire-resistant, and excellent at thermoregulation. Hemp is also a rapidly renewable resource—on 1.5 acres of land, it takes only 12 weeks for farmers to grow enough industrial hemp to build a 1,500-square-foot home. Since hemp plants enhance soil health by holding soil together and reducing erosion, farmers can grow hemp on the same parcel of land many times over. Hempcrete is also a flexible building material that allows for creative designs that can mold to a variety of aesthetic preferences. Last but not least, hempcrete is a “better-than-zero-carbon-material” (as described by [Hempcrete UK](https://www.ukhempcrete.com/)) because it traps more atmospheric carbon in the lifetime of the building than was emitted during production, transportation, and construction.

While European countries have used hempcrete for decades, the United States has been slow to adopt this sustainable concrete alternative. [According](https://hempindustrydaily.com/hempcrete-leap-lack-of-processers-standards-create-barriers-to-expanding-hemp-use-in-construction/) to Steve Allin, director of the International Hemp Building Association, since American farmers grow hemp in relatively small amounts, compared to other commodity crops, supply is the main barrier to increased hempcrete construction projects in the United States. Industrial hemp cultivation only became legal at the federal level under the *Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018* ([H.R.2](https://www.congress.gov/bill/115th-congress/house-bill/2)), which still includes restrictions on hemp growth. Asheville-based architect Anthony Brenner [explains](http://www.americanlimetechnology.com/martin-korp-residence/), “there’s a stigma that marijuana and industrial hemp are the same thing, but the THC content of industrial hemp is actually only 0.035 percent.”

Another challenge is that large-scale American hemp processing facilities are few and far between. “We have not learned of any large-scale commercial hemp decortication facility operational in the U.S.,” [wrote](https://cropwatch.unl.edu/2019/hemp-production-fiber-or-grain) Charles Wortmann and Ismail Dweikat of the University of Nebraska-Lincoln’s Institute of Agriculture and Natural Resources. Further, the [U.S.](https://ushba.org/resources/white-papers/hempcrete-certification/) [Hemp](https://ushba.org/resources/white-papers/hempcrete-certification/) [Building Association](https://ushba.org/resources/white-papers/hempcrete-certification/) is still in the process of working with the International Code Council (ICC) and the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) to “solve the permitting and performance issues that have prevented hempcrete from gaining a larger presence in the U.S.” The combination of these factors means that many builders who use hempcrete in the United States import the hemp hurd from Europe, which leads to high hempcrete costs and high CO2 emissions.

Despite these obstacles, some contractors have managed to construct hempcrete buildings in the United States. The “[Push House](http://www.americanlimetechnology.com/martin-korp-residence/),” the first hempcrete house in America, was built in 2010 for the family of the former mayor of Asheville, N.C., Ross Martin. Now, there are hemp houses in [Texas](https://www.easttexasmatters.com/news/top-stories/hemp-house-east-texas-couple-builds-home-with-hempcrete/), [Hawaii](http://nationalhempassociation.org/building-with-hemp-first-hemp-home-in-hawaii/), and [Virginia](https://www.13newsnow.com/article/news/local/mycity/virginia-beach/a-look-at-the-first-hempcrete-home-in-virginia-beach/291-522025831) and companies such as [American Hemp](https://www.americanhempllc.com/), [Left Hand Hemp](https://learnhempcrete.com/), [HempStone LLC](https://hempstone.net/), and [Hempitecture](https://www.hempitecture.com/) specialize in hempcrete architecture and construction. Still, as of 2018, only [50 homes](https://www.cnbc.com/2018/04/20/building-your-dream-home-could-send-you-to-the-hemp-dealer.html) in the United States have been built with hempcrete. One of Hempitecture’s suppliers, Sunstrand, filed for [bankruptcy](https://hempindustrydaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/Sunstrand-filing.pdf) in January of this year.

As our world becomes increasingly urbanized, the temptation will be to continue building with concrete. However, if the United States can overcome the initial policy and infrastructure hurdles, hempcrete will help open the door to a more sustainable construction industry and a greener future.

**Author: Maeve Arthur**

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Hempcrete allows for unique architectural aesthetics, enabling flexible designs that blend with modern eco-friendly themes, as seen in various innovative projects across Europe and the US.

Hempcrete enhances thermal insulation and moisture control, significantly reducing energy consumption for heating and cooling, contributing to a sustainable indoor environment and lower utility bills.
Barriers include regulatory hurdles, limited processing facilities, and misconceptions about hemp, which hinder broader adoption of hempcrete in the construction industry.
As awareness grows and regulations evolve, hempcrete could revolutionize residential building practices, aligning with sustainability goals and addressing global carbon reduction challenges.
